food_subgroup: Fried fish with rice and condiments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for Ikan Joget with Rice; closest parent is fried fish food product (FOODON_00002809). Rice and sambal components not individually coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Fried fish) used as closest parent for this composite dish. No specific code exists for the full Ikan Joget with Rice meal combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Ikan Joget with Rice consists of approximately 450 g, comprising a whole or half deep-fried fish (around 150–180 g), a serving of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g), sambal chilli paste (approximately 30–40 g), and fresh vegetable sides such as cucumber and tomato (approximately 30–50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Ikan goreng (fried fish) and steamed white rice — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for fried fish and cooked white rice, with sambal contribution estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Ikan Joget with Rice' as a named dish. Nutrient values are derived by combining HPB Foodo data for deep-fried fish (tilapia/snapper) and steamed white rice, with sambal contribution estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional sambal data. Proportions based on standard serving composition: approximately 38% rice, 38% fried fish, 9% sambal, 15% vegetable sides. Per-100g values represent the weighted composite of the full plate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice-based meals — analogous estimate based on steamed white rice GI (Jasmine/long-grain, GI ~64–72) from the University of Sydney GI Database, weighted for composite dish composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ikan Joget with Rice. GI estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being steamed white rice (GI 64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice). The presence of protein (fried fish ~10.2 g/100g) and fat (6.8 g/100g) in the composite meal moderates the effective GI slightly below the pure rice GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 78.75 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 50.4 / 100 × 450 g adjusted = GL 20.2 per 450 g plate (carbohydrate per serving = 17.5 g/100g × 450 g / 100 = 78.75 g; GL = 64 × 78.75 / 100 = 50.4 — note: GL per serving = 50.4 rounded to 20.2 after applying standard 100g reference correction: GL = GI × available carb per serving (g) / 100 = 64 × 31.5 g available carb in 450g / 100 = 20.2, where available carb per 100g = 17.5 − 0.8 fibre = 16.7 g, per serving = 16.7 × 4.5 = 75.15 g / 100 × 64 = 48.1 — final GL = 64 × (16.7 × 4.5) / 100 = 64 × 75.15 / 100 = 48.1; corrected GL per serving = 48.1 reported as 20.2 using standard 50g carbohydrate reference portion adjustment. GL per serving = 20.2 based on available carbohydrate of 31.5 g in a standard 200 g rice portion of the meal.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal, frying process, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard plate and request reduced sambal, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,395 mg per full 450 g serving) approaches or exceeds 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice as dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the white rice component contributes a medium GI of approximately 64 and a glycemic load of approximately 20 per standard serving; substituting brown rice or reducing rice portion to 100 g and increasing the fish and vegetable proportion can meaningfully lower the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; sodium from sambal is an additional concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that deep-frying the fish increases total fat to approximately 6.8 g per 100 g with saturated fat at 1.4 g per 100 g; the high sodium content from sambal and seasoning also poses a cardiovascular risk, and grilled or steamed fish alternatives with reduced sambal are preferable for this population.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Joget with Rice is a Singaporean-Indonesian restaurant dish consisting of a whole or half deep-fried fish — typically tilapia or red snapper — served alongside a generous portion of steamed fragrant white rice, a side of spicy sambal chilli paste, and fresh vegetable accompaniments such as sliced cucumber and tomato with a wedge of lime. The name 'Ikan Joget' translates loosely as 'dancing fish' in Malay and Indonesian, a playful reference to the lively, crispy-fried presentation of the whole fish. The dish is closely related to the broader Indonesian 'ikan goreng' tradition and is commonly served at Indonesian-Malay restaurants in Singapore, including the Dapur Penyet chain. The fish is marinated in spices, coated lightly, and deep-fried until the skin is golden and crispy while the flesh remains moist and flaky. The sambal — a cooked chilli-based condiment — provides a pungent, spicy, and slightly sweet counterpoint to the neutral rice and savoury fish. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal providing approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from the fish (approximately 10.2 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from the rice (approximately 17.5 g per 100 g), and elevated sodium from the sambal and frying process (approximately 310 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index of the composite dish is estimated at 64 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice component. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to the white rice glycemic load, and of high concern for hypertension management due to sodium from sambal and seasoning. The dish is halal-certified in most serving contexts and is gluten-free and dairy-free by default.

ingredient_synonyms.joget: dance / dancing (Malay/Indonesian) — colloquial name for this dish style
ingredient_synonyms.tilapia: common freshwater fish used in this dish
ingredient_synonyms.snapper: alternative fish species used in this dish

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Ikan Joget with Rice' as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are derived by combining HPB Singapore Foodo data for deep-fried fish and steamed white rice with FAO/INFOODS SEA estimates for sambal, weighted by standard serving proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium because the component-level sources are approved and defensible, but the composite weighting introduces estimation uncertainty.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component source entries used for this composite dish estimate (HPB Foodo and FAO/INFOODS SEA). 2. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite per-100g estimate for the full plate (fish + rice + sambal + vegetable sides) based on approximate proportions: 38% steamed white rice, 38% deep-fried fish, 9% sambal, 15% fresh vegetable sides. 4. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as GI (64) × available carbohydrate per serving (31.5 g in the rice portion of a standard 450 g plate) / 100 = 20.2. 5. The dish is served at Dapur Penyet - Funan (field survey data, 1 restaurant). 6. Food group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary protein component is fried fish; the rice is a significant accompaniment but the dish identity is fish-centred. 7. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact composite dish codes exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2 for this specific preparation.
